expectations.text: Detects or asks about the test framework before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Phase 1: Framework Detection runs auto-detection from project files (package.json for vitest), producing a full framework config (language: typescript, testRunner: vitest, testCommand: npx vitest run) before any Test Writer is spawned. Lines 68-98 of trace.

expectations.text: Presents a work decomposition plan and asks for user confirmation before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Phase 2 produces 3 work units (capitalize, truncate, slugify) with specs, file paths, dependencies, model assignments, and an execution plan. A 'Proceed? [confirm/modify/cancel]' prompt is shown (lines 201-233). The trace states 'User responds: confirm.' before Phase 3 begins.

expectations.text: Test Writer creates test files BEFORE any implementation code exists
expectations.evidence: For each unit, Step 4a (Test Writer) runs first and creates test files (e.g., src/__tests__/capitalize.test.ts). Step 4b (RED verification) then confirms tests fail with 'Cannot find module' errors, proving no implementation exists yet. Step 4c (Code Writer) runs only after RED verification passes. Trace lines 276-360 for capitalize unit.

expectations.text: Tests fail initially (RED phase verified) -- the skill explicitly checks for non-zero exit code
expectations.evidence: RED Verification for each unit explicitly runs the test command and checks exit code. For capitalize: 'npx vitest run src/__tests__/capitalize.test.ts 2>&1; echo EXIT_CODE:$?' yields EXIT_CODE:1 with 'Cannot find module' error. The trace says 'Exit code is 1 (non-zero). Tests FAIL. PASS.' Same pattern for truncate (EXIT_CODE:1) and slugify (EXIT_CODE:1). Lines 366-389, 746-751, 976-980.

expectations.text: Code Writer receives test file contents read from disk, not from Test Writer output (information barrier)
expectations.evidence: For each unit, the Information Barrier Verification checklist is explicitly checked: '[x] Prompt contains test file contents read from DISK (not from Test Writer output)', '[x] Prompt does NOT contain any Test Writer prompt text', '[x] Prompt does NOT contain Test Writer, Agent A, or references to test authoring process'. Lines 421-426 for capitalize, 766-767 for truncate, 993-994 for slugify.

expectations.text: Code Writer does NOT modify test files (checksum verification mentioned or performed)
expectations.evidence: GREEN Verification Check 1 for each unit computes shasum -a 256 on the test file and compares against the stored checksum from RED verification. For capitalize: 'Compare with stored checksum a1b2c3d4e5f6... Checksums match. PASS.' (line 464). Same for truncate ('Checksums match. PASS.' line 786) and slugify (line 1014). The trace also states the consequence of mismatch: 'ANTI-CHEAT VIOLATION'.

expectations.text: All tests pass after implementation (GREEN phase)
expectations.evidence: GREEN Verification Check 3 for each unit runs tests and confirms exit code 0. Capitalize: 'All 8 tests pass. EXIT_CODE:0' (line 498). Truncate: 'All 10 tests pass. EXIT_CODE:0' (line 794). Slugify: 'All 13 tests pass. EXIT_CODE:0' (line 1022).

expectations.text: An adversarial review step is performed on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Step 4f (Adversarial Review) runs for all three units with a sonnet reviewer. Each produces a structured report with scores for Test Completeness, Test Quality, Implementation Quality, Cheating Detection, and Coverage Gaps. All three receive PASS verdicts. The five anti-patterns from testing-anti-patterns.md are explicitly checked. Lines 556-619 (capitalize), 830-862 (truncate), 1061-1098 (slugify).

expectations.text: A tdd-report.md or equivalent summary is generated at the end
expectations.evidence: Phase 6: Report Generation produces tdd-report.md with full session details including summary metrics (3/3 units, 31 tests, 32 assertions, 0 violations), per-unit phase tables, anti-cheat log, and final integration check. Also generates tdd-session.jsonl. Lines 1176-1275.

expectations.text: The skill does NOT skip any verification steps even for simple specs
expectations.evidence: Despite this being a simple utility spec (3 pure functions), every verification step runs for every unit: RED verification (5 checks each), GREEN verification (4 checks each), Spec Compliance Review, Adversarial Review, Code Quality Review, and Phase 5 Final Review with full test suite run. The design gate was correctly evaluated and skipped (no trigger conditions met), but all execution-time verification steps ran. The session summary confirms: 'Three-stage review: All 3 units passed all 3 stages' (line 1315).

expectations.text: Detects or asks about the test framework before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 2-3: Claude inspects project context and picks a language/framework ad-hoc based on general heuristics. The trace states 'Claude picks a framework ad-hoc without systematic detection' and section 'Does NOT Detect Test Frameworks Systematically' confirms no systematic scan for existing test configurations across the project. No user query about framework preference occurs.

expectations.text: Presents a work decomposition plan and asks for user confirmation before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1: Claude identifies three functions but treats them as a single implementation task. Section 'Does NOT Decompose Into Work Units' states: 'Default Claude treats the three functions as a single implementation task. It does not decompose the spec into independent work units.' Section 'Does NOT Enforce a Design Gate' confirms: 'It jumps straight from parsing the request to writing the implementation.' No user confirmation step exists.

expectations.text: Test Writer creates test files BEFORE any implementation code exists
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 4 explicitly states: 'This is the critical divergence from TDD. Default Claude Code writes the implementation immediately. No tests exist.' Step 5 says tests are written AFTER implementation ('maybe'). Section 'Does NOT Write Tests First (No RED Phase)' confirms: 'The implementation is written before any test exists.'

expectations.text: Tests fail initially (RED phase verified) -- the skill explicitly checks for non-zero exit code
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Verify Tests Fail Before Implementation (No RED/GREEN Cycle)' states: 'Default Claude skips all three phases. Tests and implementation are produced in one shot or in implementation-first order. There is no verification that the tests would have failed without the implementation.' No non-zero exit code check occurs at any point.

expectations.text: Code Writer receives test file contents read from disk, not from Test Writer output (information barrier)
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Use Agent Teams or Information Barriers' states: 'Default Claude operates as a single agent in a single context. It does not spawn a Code Writer agent that receives tests from disk (not from the test-writing context). Everything happens in one context, so the entity writing tests has full knowledge of (or has just written) the implementation.'

expectations.text: Code Writer does NOT modify test files (checksum verification mentioned or performed)
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Use Agent Teams or Information Barriers' states: 'It does not enforce that the Code Writer cannot modify test files. It does not use checksum verification to detect test tampering.' Single agent writes both tests and implementation with no separation or integrity checks.

expectations.text: All tests pass after implementation (GREEN phase)
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 6 says tests would pass: 'Since the tests were written to match the implementation, they will pass.' However, there is no distinct GREEN phase -- tests were never verified to fail first, so the pass is not a meaningful RED-to-GREEN transition. The assertion table in the trace itself marks this as 'PARTIAL'. Because the expectation is about a GREEN phase (which requires a preceding RED phase), and no such phase structure exists, this fails.

expectations.text: An adversarial review step is performed on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Trace section 'Does NOT Perform Adversarial Review' explicitly lists missing checks: no verification that tests actually verify behavior, no edge case gap analysis, no check for subtle implementation bugs that could pass tests, no anti-pattern detection. No adversarial review of any kind occurs.

expectations.text: A tdd-report.md or equivalent summary is generated at the end
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 7 says Claude provides only 'a conversational summary' listing files and snippets. Section 'Does NOT Produce a Structured Report' confirms: 'Default Claude gives a conversational summary. It does not produce a structured report with per-work-unit RED/GREEN status, phase execution logs, test coverage metrics, anti-pattern scan results, adversarial review findings.' No file is generated.

expectations.text: The skill does NOT skip any verification steps even for simple specs
expectations.evidence: All verification steps are absent. The trace documents 11 distinct categories of behavior that default Claude does NOT perform, including: no RED phase, no GREEN verification cycle, no information barrier, no adversarial review, no spec compliance review, no anti-pattern detection, no design gate, no state management, no decomposition, no systematic framework detection, and no structured reporting. Every verification step is skipped.

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) -- asks clarifying questions about the auth system before decomposing
expectations.evidence: Phase 0 Design Gate is triggered. The trace evaluates trigger conditions and finds 3 of 4 fire: '3+ distinct features/components: YES - registration, login, password reset = 3 distinct features', 'External integrations: YES - JWT tokens, email notification, password hashing imply external dependencies', 'Ambiguous about data flow: YES - How do failed attempts interact with account lockout?...' Three clarifying questions are asked one at a time: (1) password reset token expiry duration, (2) consequence of failed login attempts, (3) sync vs async email notification. Lines 53-87.

expectations.text: Produces a design summary with components, data flow, and key decisions before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Step 3 of the Design Gate produces a structured design summary with sections for Components (UserRegistration, UserLogin, PasswordReset), Data Flow (4 flows for registration, login, password-reset-request, password-reset-execute), Key Decisions (JWT + refresh tokens, bcrypt, account lockout params, async email, session invalidation on reset), and Out of Scope (email verification, OAuth, MFA, RBAC, rate limiting). Lines 111-147. The summary is stored in state under designSummary and passed to Test Writers.

expectations.text: Decomposes into at least 3 work units (registration, login, password-reset or similar)
expectations.evidence: Phase 2 Work Decomposition produces exactly 3 work units: (1) user-registration with spec for email validation, bcrypt hashing, duplicate detection; (2) user-login with spec for JWT access/refresh tokens, failed attempt tracking, lockout; (3) password-reset with spec for token generation, TTL, password update, refresh token invalidation, async email. Lines 206-238.

expectations.text: Identifies dependencies between units (login depends on registration, password-reset depends on registration)
expectations.evidence: Dependency analysis explicitly states: 'user-registration: Independent. No dependencies on other units.' 'user-login: Depends on user-registration. Login needs a registered user to authenticate against.' 'password-reset: Depends on user-registration. Password reset needs an existing user...' Work plan shows Dependencies: none for registration, Dependencies: [user-registration] for login and password-reset. Lines 182-190 and 225-233.

expectations.text: Respects dependency order -- implements registration before login and password-reset
expectations.evidence: Execution plan is: 'Batch 1: user-registration' followed by 'Batch 2 (parallel): user-login, password-reset'. Batch 2 agents are only spawned after Batch 1 completes: 'Both units dependencies ([user-registration]) are now in Batch 1, which is complete. Per execution.maxParallelPairs: 3 and parallelMode: auto, both units execute concurrently.' Lines 234-237 and 506-508.

expectations.text: Each work unit goes through the full RED->GREEN->review cycle independently
expectations.evidence: For user-registration: Step 4a Test Writer -> Step 4b RED Verification (5 checks) -> Step 4c Code Writer -> Step 4d GREEN Verification (4 checks) -> Step 4e Spec Compliance -> Step 4f Adversarial Review -> Step 4g Code Quality Review. Lines 271-503. For user-login: same pipeline traced at lines 514-587. For password-reset: same pipeline traced at lines 592-659. Each unit independently goes through all phases before being marked completed.

expectations.text: Runs a final integration test across all units after individual completion
expectations.evidence: Phase 5 Final Review runs after all units complete. Step 1: 'Run Full Test Suite: npx vitest run 2>&1; echo EXIT_CODE:$?' -- runs ALL test files together. Step 4 is an explicit 'Cross-Unit Integration Check' that verifies: 'Does user-login correctly use the user model from user-registration?', 'Does password-reset correctly invalidate refresh tokens created by user-login?', 'Do the bcrypt calls in user-registration and password-reset use consistent settings?', 'Are shared types/interfaces compatible?' Lines 667-714.

expectations.text: The report shows all work units with their individual phase statuses
expectations.evidence: Phase 6 generates tdd-report.md with a summary table (3/3 work units, ~30-40 tests, ~50-70 assertions, 0 violations, 3/3 adversarial reviews passed, 0 retries) and per-unit sections. Each unit (user-registration, user-login, password-reset) has a table with Phase/Status/Attempts/Notes columns covering Test Writer, RED Verification, Code Writer, GREEN Verification, Spec Compliance, Adversarial Review, and Code Quality. Lines 720-774.

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) — asks clarifying questions about the auth system before decomposing
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1 explicitly states: 'Would it ask design questions first? No. Default Claude Code does not gate on design review.' The trace lists seven questions that would NOT be asked (database choice, JWT algorithm, password policy, lockout thresholds, email transport, app type, token expiry). Summary confirms: 'Claude Code would not ask a single clarifying question. It would infer defaults for every ambiguous decision.' No design gate was triggered.

expectations.text: Produces a design summary with components, data flow, and key decisions before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Produces design summary with components, data flow, key decisions — No. No design document produced.' The trace shows Claude Code went directly from intake (Step 1) to technology detection (Step 2) to implementation (Step 3) with no intermediate design artifact. The 'What Default Claude Code Does NOT Do' section under '1. No Design Gate' confirms: 'No data flow diagram or component identification.'

expectations.text: Decomposes into at least 3 work units (registration, login, password-reset or similar)
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Decomposes into at least 3 work units — No. No formal decomposition; features built linearly.' While Claude Code did implement three features (registration in Step 3c, login in Step 3d, password reset in Step 3e), these were not formalized as discrete work units with acceptance criteria, inputs/outputs, or tracked status. The 'No Formal Decomposition' section confirms: 'No work unit definitions with clear inputs, outputs, and acceptance criteria. No dependency graph. No tracking of unit completion status.'

expectations.text: Identifies dependencies between units (login depends on registration, password-reset depends on registration)
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Identifies dependencies between units — No. Dependencies are implicit in build order only.' The 'No Formal Decomposition' section confirms: 'No dependency graph (e.g., "login depends on registration because it needs user lookup"). No parallelization analysis.' Dependencies exist in the code (login needs a registered user) but are never explicitly identified or documented.

expectations.text: Respects dependency order — implements registration before login and password-reset
expectations.evidence: The trace shows registration (Step 3c) was implemented before login (Step 3d) and password reset (Step 3e), so the natural ordering is correct. However, the trace evaluation table rates this as only 'Partially' met, noting the order is 'by convention, not tracked formally.' There is no explicit dependency analysis driving the order. Grading as FAIL because the ordering is incidental rather than the result of dependency-aware planning, which is what the expectation describes ('Respects dependency order' implies a deliberate mechanism). The trace itself only awards 0.5 partial credit across all 8 assertions.

expectations.text: Each work unit goes through the full RED→GREEN→review cycle independently
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Each work unit goes through RED->GREEN->review cycle — No. No RED phase; tests written after all code.' Step 4 confirms: 'Default Claude Code writes tests after implementation is complete. The tests are shaped by what was built, not by what was specified.' The 'No RED/GREEN Verification' section adds: 'There is no RED/GREEN/REFACTOR cycle. Tests are never run in a failing state to confirm they detect missing functionality.' All three features were implemented monolithically before any test was written.

expectations.text: Runs a final integration test across all units after individual completion
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Runs final integration test across all units — No. Only runs jest once at the end; no dedicated integration pass.' Step 5 shows a single 'npx jest --verbose' run that executes all tests together, but this is not a deliberate integration test phase — it is the only test run, occurring after all implementation and test-writing is complete. There is no separation between unit tests and integration tests, and no cross-feature integration scenarios.

expectations.text: The report shows all work units with their individual phase statuses
expectations.evidence: Trace evaluation table states: 'Report shows all work units with individual phase statuses — No. Output is conversational text, not a structured report.' Step 6 confirms the final output is: 'a conversational summary ("I built an auth system with these endpoints..."). This summary is unstructured text, not a formal report.' The 'No Structured Report' section adds: 'No work unit status table. No coverage metrics breakdown per feature. No spec compliance matrix.'

expectations.text: Detects that the user provided an existing test file (entry point mode 3)
expectations.evidence: Step 2: 'The phrase "implement against" combined with a test file path matches Mode 3.' The trace explicitly states: 'Result: entryPoint = "user-provided-test"'. Lines 39-46.

expectations.text: Skips the Test Writer phase -- does NOT rewrite or replace the user's test file
expectations.evidence: Step 3b explicitly states: 'Test Writer -- SKIPPED. No Test Writer agent spawned. The file src/__tests__/calculator.test.ts exists on disk.' The Mode 3 Phase Execution summary table confirms: 'Step 4a: Test Writer -- SKIPPED -- Mode 3 explicit skip (SKILL.md line 114)'. Lines 61-63 and 611. No Test Writer is ever spawned in the entire trace.

expectations.text: Reads the existing test file from disk and passes contents to the Code Writer
expectations.evidence: Step 9 states: 'The orchestrator reads the test file FROM DISK (not from any agent output -- the information barrier).' The Code Writer prompt includes '{test_file_contents_verbatim}: (contents of src/__tests__/calculator.test.ts read from disk)'. The Information Barrier Checklist explicitly checks: '[x] Test file contents read from DISK'. Lines 159-198.

expectations.text: Still performs GREEN verification (checksum check, all tests pass)
expectations.evidence: Step 10 GREEN Verification runs fully in Mode 3 with 4 checks: Check 1 compares test file checksum against stored value from Step 8; Check 2 greps for skip/focus markers; Check 3 runs the test command and requires exit code 0; Check 4 checks for hardcoded returns. Lines 206-231. The Mode 3 Phase Execution table confirms: 'Step 4d: GREEN Verification -- RUNS -- Checksum + skip markers + all-tests-pass'. Line 615.

expectations.text: Still performs adversarial review on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Step 12 describes the full Adversarial Review (Stage 2 of the three-stage pipeline) with inputs (spec-contract, test file from disk, impl file from disk, scoring rubric), all 5 review categories (Test Completeness, Test Quality, Implementation Quality, Cheating Detection, Coverage Gaps), and the 5 anti-patterns from testing-anti-patterns.md. The Mode 3 Phase Execution table confirms: 'Step 4f: Adversarial Review -- RUNS -- Stage 2; only after spec compliance passes'. Lines 314-376 and 616.

expectations.text: Does NOT ask the user to confirm a work decomposition plan for a single provided test
expectations.evidence: Step 6 explicitly states: 'No confirmation dialog: A single user-provided test file produces one work unit. The multi-unit "Proceed? [confirm/modify/cancel]" dialog (SKILL.md lines 203-229) is not shown -- it would be redundant noise.' Lines 126-127.

expectations.text: Detects that the user provided an existing test file (entry point mode 3)
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states: 'Default Claude Code has no concept of modes. It just reads the file and writes code.' and 'Detect this as "entry point mode 3" (user-provided test) -- it has no mode concept' under 'What Claude Code would NOT do'. Summary confirms: 'FAIL -- no mode concept'. The baseline treats the prompt as a generic natural-language instruction with no structured mode detection.

expectations.text: Skips the Test Writer phase -- does NOT rewrite or replace the user's test file
expectations.evidence: Trace rates this PARTIAL: 'Claude Code would not proactively rewrite the tests, but it has no hard protection against modifying them under pressure.' Specifically, the trace warns Claude Code may 'Adjust assertions it believes are "incorrect"', 'Change import paths', 'Add .skip or .todo to problematic tests', or 'Rewrite test descriptions' if the implementation proves difficult. There is no Test Writer agent to skip (vacuously true), but the key protection -- treating the user's test file as an immutable contract -- is absent. The lack of checksum enforcement or skip-marker detection means test file integrity is not guaranteed. Graded FAIL because the assertion requires the test file to be reliably preserved, not merely likely preserved.

expectations.text: Reads the existing test file from disk and passes contents to the Code Writer
expectations.evidence: Trace confirms the file is read: 'Claude Code WOULD read the test file. When a user references a specific file path, Claude Code reliably reads it.' However, the assertion implies the TDD skill's architecture: a separate Code Writer agent receiving the test contents through an information barrier. The trace states: 'there is no separate Code Writer agent and no information barrier. The same context that reads the tests also writes the implementation.' Summary confirms: 'PARTIAL -- reads file, but no separate agent'. Graded FAIL because the pass-to-Code-Writer component is entirely absent; reading the file alone does not satisfy the structured handoff this assertion describes.

expectations.text: Still performs GREEN verification (checksum check, all tests pass)
expectations.evidence: Trace states: 'No checksum verification whatsoever. If the test file was modified, nobody would notice.' For test execution: 'Claude Code would LIKELY run the tests after writing the implementation' but 'this is NOT guaranteed' -- it may 'Skip running tests if it judges the implementation to be obviously correct' or 'Say "the tests should pass now" without actually running them.' The trace also notes 'No check for newly added skip/ignore markers in tests.' Summary confirms: 'FAIL -- no checksum; test run likely but not enforced'. Both components of GREEN verification (checksum integrity and mandatory test pass) are absent or unreliable.

expectations.text: Still performs adversarial review on the test+implementation pair
expectations.evidence: Trace is unambiguous: 'Default Claude Code performs no independent review. The same agent that wrote the code would need to critique it -- a fundamental conflict of interest. No anti-pattern detection, no scoring rubric, no structured review output.' Summary confirms: 'FAIL -- no independent review'. There is no separate reviewer agent, no edge-case analysis, no cheating detection, and no structured evaluation of the test+implementation pair.

expectations.text: Does NOT ask the user to confirm a work decomposition plan for a single provided test
expectations.evidence: Trace states: 'Default Claude Code would also not ask for a work decomposition plan because it has no concept of work decomposition. It would just start implementing. This is coincidentally correct behavior for this assertion.' Summary confirms: 'PASS -- coincidentally correct'. The baseline proceeds directly to implementation without prompting for plan confirmation, satisfying the assertion even though the reason differs from the TDD skill's intentional design.

expectations.text: Detects entry point mode 2 -- existing codebase coverage mode
expectations.evidence: Step 0 parses the specification text as 'add test coverage for src/utils/' and Step 2 states: 'The phrase "add test coverage for" triggers Entry Point Mode 2 (Existing Codebase).' Line 19.

expectations.text: Reads and analyzes the existing source files in src/utils/ before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Step 5 (Phase 2 Work Decomposition) describes Mode 2 adaptation: '5a: Scan source directory -- List all source files in src/utils/' finding formatDate.ts, parseConfig.ts, stringHelpers.ts. '5b: Read each source file -- For each file, read the code to understand: Exported functions/classes and their signatures, Observable behavior, Edge cases, Side effects.' '5c: Produce work units' with reverse-engineered spec-contracts derived from reading the source. Lines 95-129.

expectations.text: Skips the design gate because --skip-design flag was passed
expectations.evidence: Step 3 explicitly states: 'Decision: SKIP. Three independent reasons to skip: 1. Mode 2 (existing codebase) skips design gate by default. 2. --skip-design flag explicitly requests skipping. 3. Characterizing existing code does not require architectural design.' designSummary is set to null. Lines 54-65.

expectations.text: Generates tests derived from reading specific source file contents (function signatures, return types, edge cases) -- not just generic tests
expectations.evidence: Step 5b describes reading each source file to understand 'Exported functions/classes and their signatures, Observable behavior (inputs -> outputs), Edge cases visible in the code (error handling, boundary checks), Side effects.' Step 5c produces spec-contracts that are 'reverse-engineered from existing code' -- e.g., for format-date: 'Characterize the formatDate function. It accepts a Date object and an optional format string. It returns a formatted date string. Currently handles ISO, short, and long formats. Throws on invalid Date input.' Step 7a confirms the Test Writer receives 'a spec-contract derived from reading the existing source file (reverse-engineered behavior spec)'. Lines 106-129 and 200-210.

expectations.text: Describes or resolves the tension between RED verification and existing implementations (characterization tests pass immediately against existing code)
expectations.evidence: Step 7b opens with: 'This is the critical Mode 2 adaptation. Standard RED verification requires tests to FAIL. In coverage mode, the implementation already exists, so characterization tests that PASS immediately is expected, not a violation.' The trace explicitly describes the tension and the resolution: characterization tests passing against existing code is correct behavior, not a cheat. Lines 224-227.

expectations.text: Demonstrates the adapted hide-and-restore RED verification procedure for coverage mode (temporarily hide impl, verify import failures, restore, verify pass)
expectations.evidence: Step 7b describes the full 4-step hide-and-restore procedure: Step 1: 'mv src/utils/formatDate.ts src/utils/formatDate.ts.tdd-hidden' (hide implementation). Step 2: Run tests -- 'they SHOULD FAIL' with import/module errors (if tests pass = ANTI-CHEAT VIOLATION: tautological tests). Step 3: 'mv src/utils/formatDate.ts.tdd-hidden src/utils/formatDate.ts' (restore -- CRITICAL: must happen regardless of outcome). Step 4: Run tests -- 'they SHOULD PASS' (if fail = characterization error). A detailed decision tree and flowchart diagram are provided. Lines 237-288 and 688-728.

expectations.text: Produces a report showing coverage additions
expectations.evidence: Step 10a generates tdd-report.md with Entry Point: existing-codebase, summary metrics (3/3 work units, test count, assertion count, adversarial reviews), and per-unit sections for Format Date, Parse Config, and String Helpers showing all phase statuses. The report notes Code Writer as '(skipped)' where applicable. Lines 610-655.

expectations.text: Detects entry point mode 2 — existing codebase coverage mode
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1 states: 'No skill is invoked. No structured pipeline begins. No entry point mode detection occurs. Claude does not classify this as "coverage mode" or any other formal mode.' The summary confirms: 'No mode classification exists in default Claude.' Default Claude treats the input as natural language and has no concept of mode routing.

expectations.text: Reads and analyzes the existing source files in src/utils/ before writing tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 2 documents that Claude reads each source file: Read('src/utils/stringHelpers.ts'), Read('src/utils/dateUtils.ts'), Read('src/utils/validators.ts'), Read('src/utils/apiClient.ts'), plus checks package.json for the test runner. The trace acknowledges the reading occurs but qualifies it as 'shallow' — no structured analysis, no spec-contracts, no dependency mapping, no work-unit decomposition. The assertion as worded ('reads and analyzes') is satisfied at a basic level.

expectations.text: Skips the design gate because --skip-design flag was passed
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 1: 'The --skip-design flag has no semantic meaning to default Claude — it is either ignored entirely or loosely interpreted.' Assertion analysis in the trace: 'Default Claude has no design gate concept. There is no gate to skip. The absence of a design phase is incidental to default behavior, not a response to the --skip-design flag. Claude would behave identically with or without the flag.' The flag is not parsed or acknowledged; the behavior is incidental, not causal.

expectations.text: Generates tests derived from reading specific source file contents (function signatures, return types, edge cases) — not just generic tests
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 3 describes how Claude generates tests referencing actual function names (capitalize, slugify), actual parameter types, and actual return values. The tests are not generic smoke tests. However, the trace qualifies this heavily: 'tests mirror internal branches rather than specifying expected behavior independently,' and 'Edge cases are copied from visible code paths, not reasoned about from the function contract.' Granting a pass because the assertion explicitly asks for tests derived from 'reading specific source file contents (function signatures, return types, edge cases)' which is what implementation-coupled generation does — it reads specific contents and derives tests from them, even if the derivation method is not behavioral.

expectations.text: Describes or resolves the tension between RED verification and existing implementations (characterization tests pass immediately against existing code)
expectations.evidence: Trace assertion analysis: 'Default Claude has no concept of RED verification. It does not recognize the tension between "tests should fail first" and "implementation already exists." It does not mention, describe, or resolve this tension. It simply writes tests that pass and moves on.' The summary adds: 'No concept of RED verification or the tension it creates in coverage mode.' There is zero discussion of this conceptual challenge anywhere in the baseline behavior.

expectations.text: Demonstrates the adapted hide-and-restore RED verification procedure for coverage mode (temporarily hide impl, verify import failures, restore, verify pass)
expectations.evidence: Trace assertion analysis: 'Default Claude would never: 1) Temporarily rename/move implementation files, 2) Run tests to verify they fail with import errors, 3) Restore the implementation files, 4) Run tests to verify they pass with the implementation present. This four-step verification procedure is entirely absent from default Claude Code behavior.' The summary identifies this as the single strongest differentiator: 'entirely absent without the skill; a novel procedure that proves tests are not tautological when implementation pre-exists.'

expectations.text: Produces a report showing coverage additions
expectations.evidence: Trace Step 5 describes the output as 'a conversational summary' with a bulleted list. The trace explicitly lists what is absent: 'No assertion density metrics, No coverage percentage or coverage gap analysis, No anti-pattern flags, No work unit breakdown with status, No session log or event timeline, No machine-readable format, No verification evidence, No checksums or anti-cheat data.' The summary confirms: 'Only informal conversational summary, no metrics or structured output.'

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) due to ambiguity
expectations.evidence: Step 3 evaluates trigger conditions and finds THREE fire: '3+ distinct features/components: YES -- payments, credit cards, refunds = 3 components', 'External integrations: YES -- Credit card processing inherently requires a payment gateway/processor', 'Ambiguous about data flow/ownership/error handling: YES -- like credit cards and stuff (no defined payment methods), also refunds maybe? (uncertain scope), idk the details yet (explicit admission of ambiguity)'. Result: 'THREE trigger conditions fire. Design gate is MANDATORY.' Lines 49-75.

expectations.text: Asks clarifying questions about the payment system -- does NOT assume specific technologies or providers
expectations.evidence: Phase 0 Step 1 asks 3 questions. Question 1: 'What payment provider should this integrate with -- Stripe, a different provider, or should it be provider-agnostic with an adapter layer?' -- presents options without assuming. Question 2: 'When you say refunds, should the system support full refunds only, or also partial refunds? And should refunds be automatic or require manual approval?' Question 3: 'What information do you need to track per payment -- just the charge ID and status, or full details?' The skill waits for user responses before proceeding. Lines 81-109.

expectations.text: Asks questions one at a time, not as a dump of 10 questions at once
expectations.evidence: The trace explicitly states: 'Ask the user 1-3 targeted questions about ambiguities. Ask one at a time -- do not dump a questionnaire.' Each question is presented individually with a '[BLOCKED -- waiting for user response]' marker between them. Question 1 is asked, user responds, then Question 2 is asked, user responds, then Question 3. Lines 83-109. Three questions total, asked sequentially.

expectations.text: Does NOT start writing code or tests before clarifying the spec
expectations.evidence: Phase 0 (Design Gate) runs entirely before Phase 1 (Framework Detection), Phase 2 (Work Decomposition), Phase 3 (State Initialization), and Phase 4 (Agent Team Orchestration). The trace shows the complete sequence: Step 0 prerequisites -> Step 1 argument parsing -> Step 2 config loading -> Step 3 design gate evaluation -> Phase 0 clarifying questions + trade-offs + design summary + user approval -> Phase 1 framework detection -> Phase 2 decomposition -> Phase 3 state init -> Phase 4 agent orchestration. No Test Writer or Code Writer is spawned until Phase 4, which begins at line 339. The 'Key Observations' section confirms: 'Three Separate User Checkpoints Before Any Code' and 'No code was written until the user approved all three.' Lines 520-526.

expectations.text: Proposes design approaches with trade-offs (e.g., Stripe vs custom, sync vs async processing)
expectations.evidence: Phase 0 Step 2 presents a formal trade-off proposal titled 'Key Architectural Decision: Payment Data Persistence Strategy' with three options: Option A (Stripe as source of truth -- thin wrapper) with pros/cons, Option B (Local record store with Stripe sync -- dual-write) with pros/cons, Option C (Event-sourced payment log) with pros/cons, and a recommendation for Option B. The trace's Key Observations section emphasizes: 'This was executed as a separate, formal proposal with three labeled options (A, B, C), each with structured pros and cons, and a recommendation. It was NOT: A question with options embedded, A passing mention of alternatives, Skipped because the user probably wants the obvious choice.' Lines 113-168 and 511-518.

expectations.text: Waits for user approval of the design before decomposing into work units
expectations.evidence: Phase 0 Step 4 presents 'Proceed with this design? [confirm/modify/cancel]' with '[BLOCKED -- waiting for user response]' and the explicit statement: 'HARD GATE: No decomposition (Phase 2) until the design is approved. If the user modifies, iterate. If cancelled, stop.' Only after user responds 'looks good, proceed' does the trace say 'Design approved. Store the design summary in state file under designSummary. Proceed to Phase 1.' Phase 2 Work Decomposition follows at line 253. Lines 213-225.

expectations.text: Triggers the design gate (Phase 0) due to ambiguity
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states: 'No design gate exists' and 'No design gate: Claude would not produce a formal design summary and wait for user approval before proceeding. There is no hard gate.' (trace lines 85-86). The summary confirms: 'Ambiguity detection triggers design gate -- No -- vagueness filled with defaults.' The baseline has no concept of a Phase 0 design gate. The vague phrasing 'idk the details yet' is not detected as an ambiguity signal that should trigger structured clarification.

expectations.text: Asks clarifying questions about the payment system -- does NOT assume specific technologies or providers
expectations.evidence: Trace describes two likely paths: (1) 70-80% probability path where Claude 'skips clarification entirely' and 'Jump straight to implementation', silently assuming Stripe, TypeScript, REST API; (2) 20-30% path where questions are asked but in a batch dump. In both paths, Claude assumes specific technologies -- the trace states: 'Pick a payment provider (almost certainly Stripe, as it dominates training data)' (line 32) and 'Assume Stripe as the payment provider without asking' (line 73). Summary confirms: 'Technology-agnostic clarification -- No -- assumes Stripe, TS, REST'.

expectations.text: Asks questions one at a time, not as a dump of 10 questions at once
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly identifies the 'question dump' anti-pattern: in the less likely path (20-30%), Claude would deliver '8-10 questions at once' (lines 43-54 show a sample batch of 10 questions). The trace states: 'This is the "question dump" anti-pattern -- 10 questions at once rather than a structured, progressive clarification flow.' (lines 55-56). In the dominant 70-80% path, no questions are asked at all. Neither path asks questions one at a time.

expectations.text: Does NOT start writing code or tests before clarifying the spec
expectations.evidence: The trace's dominant path (70-80% probability) is: 'Jump straight to implementation' (line 28). Claude 'would interpret the vague request, make assumptions, and produce' a full payment service module (lines 29-36). Step 2 ('Assumed Implementation') describes the files and code Claude would generate without any clarification. The summary confirms: 'Claude would skip clarification entirely and jump straight to implementation.' Even in the minority path where questions are asked, there is no hard gate preventing code generation before answers are received.

expectations.text: Proposes design approaches with trade-offs (e.g., Stripe vs custom, sync vs async processing)
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states under 'What Would NOT Happen': 'No trade-off analysis: Claude would not present options like "Stripe vs. custom processor" or "sync vs. async payment processing" with trade-offs for each.' (lines 89-90). Instead, Claude 'Picks one approach silently' and 'Assume a specific architecture (service class pattern) without discussing alternatives' (line 74). The summary confirms: 'Design summary with trade-offs -- No -- single approach, no alternatives'.

expectations.text: Waits for user approval of the design before decomposing into work units
expectations.evidence: Trace explicitly states: 'No spec approval checkpoint: Claude would not wait for the user to say "yes, this design looks right" before writing code.' (lines 104-105). There is also 'No work unit decomposition: The entire payment system would be implemented in one pass, not broken into small, independently testable units.' (lines 95-96). The end state (Step 4) shows Claude delivering a completed module and only then asking 'Let me know if you want me to adjust anything!' -- post-hoc review rather than pre-approval. Summary confirms: 'Hard gate on user approval -- No -- proceeds without approval'.

expectations.text: Detects entry point mode 4 (plan execution)
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 1 maps the input '/tdd execute docs/plan.md' against all four mode patterns in a table and correctly matches Mode 4 via SKILL.md line 106 ('execute plan...' or provides a plan file). The trace then enumerates all six Mode 4 adaptations (task extraction, classification, dependency ordering, design gate skip, implementer subagent, status handling) that are applied. This matches SKILL.md lines 99-107 and 122-131 exactly.

expectations.text: Classifies tasks into code (registration, login) and non-code (migration, config, docs) types
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 2 presents a classification table with all 5 tasks. Registration and login are classified as 'code' (application logic needing behavioral tests). Migration, env-config, and API docs are classified as 'task' (non-code). Each classification includes a rationale referencing both SKILL.md line 204 ('whether the task produces code that needs tests') and specific line numbers in reference/implementer-prompt.md that enumerate non-code categories (migrations, configs, docs).

expectations.text: Routes code tasks through the full TDD pipeline (Test Writer -> RED -> Code Writer -> GREEN -> Review)
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 3 details the 7-step pipeline for code tasks (Tasks 2 and 3): Step 4a Test Writer, Step 4b RED Verification (5 checks + checksums), Step 4c Code Writer (information barrier enforced), Step 4d GREEN Verification (4 checks), Step 4e Spec Compliance Review, Step 4f Adversarial Review, Step 4g Code Quality Review. Section 8 (execution timeline) walks through the identical pipeline for both user-registration (Batch 2) and user-login (Batch 3) with state transitions at each step.

expectations.text: Routes non-code tasks through implementer dispatch (Implementer -> Spec Compliance -> Code Quality)
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 3 under 'Non-Code Task Pipeline' describes the 3-step dispatch for Tasks 1, 4, and 5: Implementer spawn (Step 4h with inline task text), Spec Compliance Review (Stage 1), Code Quality Review (Stage 2). The trace explicitly notes adversarial review is skipped for non-code tasks, citing SKILL.md line 469. Section 6 explains the rationale: adversarial review is TDD-specific (cheating detection, mock analysis, test-implementation coupling) and none of those checks apply to migrations, configs, or docs.

expectations.text: Respects dependency ordering -- migration before registration, registration before login, config before login, both before docs
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 4 shows the full dependency graph and topological sort producing 4 batches: Batch 1 (parallel: database-migration, env-config -- both have no dependencies), Batch 2 (user-registration -- depends on migration in Batch 1), Batch 3 (user-login -- depends on registration Batch 2 and env-config Batch 1, so max+1 = Batch 3), Batch 4 (api-docs -- depends on registration Batch 2 and login Batch 3, so max+1 = Batch 4). The execution timeline in Section 8 confirms batch completion is a hard gate: 'Wait: Both Task 1 and Task 4 must fully complete (including reviews) before Batch 2 starts.' All five dependency constraints are satisfied.

expectations.text: Presents a work plan showing task types and execution batches before proceeding
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 4 under 'User Confirmation' (SKILL.md lines 219-296) presents a complete work plan block with: framework, mode (plan-execution), parallel config, work unit count (5 total, 2 code, 3 task), each unit listed with its type tag ([code] or [task]), spec, files, and dependencies, followed by a 4-batch execution plan with dependency annotations. Ends with 'Proceed? [confirm/modify/cancel]' and states 'The orchestrator waits for user confirmation before proceeding.'

expectations.text: Applies the subagent status protocol (DONE/DONE_WITH_CONCERNS/NEEDS_CONTEXT/BLOCKED) to all agents
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 5 presents the 4-status protocol in a table with orchestrator actions for each status. It then details status handling per agent type (Test Writer, Code Writer, Implementer) -- all three agent types use the identical four statuses with type-specific response guidance. The critical rule is quoted: 'Never ignore an escalation or force the same subagent to retry without changes.' Implementer-specific escalation guidance from reference/implementer-prompt.md is also cited (lines 66-73, 79-83, 88-95). Section 8 timeline shows status reporting at every agent spawn point.

expectations.text: Runs a final holistic review across all tasks after individual completion
expectations.evidence: Trace Section 7 describes Phase 5 (Final Holistic Review) triggered after all 5 work units across all 4 batches complete. Five steps are enumerated: (1) full test suite run -- all files together, not individually, (2) pristine output verification -- no warnings/skips/pending, (3) holistic code review across all units -- with scenario-specific examples (shared types between registration/login, JWT config consumption, migration schema matching), (4) cross-unit integration check with all 5 dependency relationships verified, (5) fix with evidence if issues found. The Iron Law and anti-rationalization table (5 excuses with responses) are included. This matches SKILL.md lines 526-549.

expectations.text: Detects entry point mode 4 (plan execution)
expectations.evidence: No mode detection system exists. The trace states: 'No mode detection system; treats `/tdd execute` as natural language.' Claude treats the entire prompt as a natural language request and has no concept of entry point modes.

expectations.text: Classifies tasks into code (registration, login) and non-code (migration, config, docs) types
expectations.evidence: The trace explicitly states: 'No formal classification. Claude Code does not distinguish "code task" from "non-code task" as a routing decision.' All 5 tasks are handled identically by a single agent using the same approach.

expectations.text: Routes code tasks through the full TDD pipeline (Test Writer -> RED -> Code Writer -> GREEN -> Review)
expectations.evidence: Code tasks (registration, login) are implemented code-first with tests written afterward. The trace states: 'No. Claude writes the registration endpoint with email validation logic, then writes tests afterward.' No Test Writer agent, no RED verification, no Code Writer behind an information barrier, no review stage.

expectations.text: Routes non-code tasks through implementer dispatch (Implementer -> Spec Compliance -> Code Quality)
expectations.evidence: Non-code tasks (migration, config, docs) are written directly with no review pipeline. The trace states: 'No implementer dispatch; non-code tasks written directly with no review.' No Spec Compliance or Code Quality reviewers are invoked.

expectations.text: Respects dependency ordering -- migration before registration, registration before login, config before login, both before docs
expectations.evidence: Critical dependency violation: Task 3 (login with JWT) executes before Task 4 (environment config for JWT_SECRET). The trace shows execution order 1,2,3,4,5 which violates the constraint that config (Task 4) must precede login (Task 3). The trace states: 'Task 3 is executed before Task 4 (dependency violation -- login depends on config).' Other dependencies are satisfied only accidentally by sequential plan-file ordering, not by formal dependency resolution.

expectations.text: Presents a work plan showing task types and execution batches before proceeding
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No work plan presented; jumps straight to execution.' Claude begins executing tasks immediately after reading the plan file. No preview of task classifications, batch ordering, or dependency graph is shown to the user.

expectations.text: Applies the subagent status protocol (DONE/DONE_WITH_CONCERNS/NEEDS_CONTEXT/BLOCKED) to all agents
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No status protocol; tasks complete silently.' No DONE, DONE_WITH_CONCERNS, NEEDS_CONTEXT, or BLOCKED signals are emitted. Problems are silently worked around and concerns are not flagged.

expectations.text: Runs a final holistic review across all tasks after individual completion
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No holistic review; only a batch test run at the end.' A single `npx jest --verbose` run occurs at the end, but no cross-cutting review checks consistency between migration schema and registration code, JWT config and login implementation, or API docs and actual endpoints.

expectations.text: Decomposes into 4 work units with no dependencies between them
expectations.evidence: Trace Phase 2.1 shows 4 units (get-users, post-users, get-user-by-id, delete-user-by-id) each with dependsOn: []. Phase 2.2 dependency analysis explicitly states 'No unit imports types or functions from another unit' and concludes 'dependsOn: [] for all 4 units. All are independent.' The unit table at lines 100-108 confirms all four have empty dependency arrays.

expectations.text: Computes a single parallel batch containing all 4 units
expectations.evidence: Trace Phase 2.3 shows the topological sort: all 4 nodes have in-degree 0, placed into Batch 1, no remaining nodes. Phase 2.4 states 'Result: Single batch containing all 4 units.' The execution plan reads 'Batch 1 (parallel): get-users, post-users, get-user-by-id, delete-user-by-id' with no Batch 2. Summary confirms: 'Topological sort: All 4 nodes have in-degree 0, so all are placed in Batch 1. The sort terminates after one pass -- no Batch 2 exists.'

expectations.text: Presents the execution plan showing all units run concurrently in Batch 1
expectations.evidence: Trace Phase 2.5 shows the full user confirmation prompt including 'Execution plan: Batch 1 (parallel): get-users, post-users, get-user-by-id, delete-user-by-id' and 'Proceed? [confirm/modify/cancel]'. The plan also includes per-unit breakdowns showing 'Dependencies: none' for each unit, plus 'Parallel: auto (max 3 concurrent)' and 'Work units: 4'.

expectations.text: Dispatches multiple Test Writers simultaneously (up to maxParallelPairs config, default 3)
expectations.evidence: Trace Phase 4.1 shows maxParallelPairs=3 and 4 units in the batch. The dispatch timeline at lines 247-251 shows: T=0 dispatches 3 Test Writers concurrently (get-users slot 1/3, post-users slot 2/3, get-user-by-id slot 3/3) while delete-user-by-id is QUEUED. When the first Test Writer completes at T=0:45, delete-user-by-id is dequeued into the freed slot. The concurrency timeline visualization at lines 633-652 confirms the 3+1 pattern.

expectations.text: Each unit independently goes through the full RED->GREEN->review cycle
expectations.evidence: Trace Phase 4.2 shows each unit progressing independently through its full pipeline: Test Writer -> RED verification (5-step check including test existence, failure confirmation, assertion density, behavior scan, checksum) -> Code Writer -> GREEN verification (checksum compare, skip/focus scan, test run, hardcoded return scan) -> three-stage review. The trace explicitly demonstrates pipeline independence: 'as each Test Writer finishes, its unit immediately enters RED verification while other Test Writers may still be running.' For example, get-users enters RED at T=0:45 while post-users and get-user-by-id Test Writers are still running.

expectations.text: Applies the three-stage review pipeline: spec compliance -> adversarial -> code quality
expectations.evidence: Trace Phase 4.3 explicitly applies the three-stage pipeline per the ORDERING RULE (SKILL.md line 457). Stage 1 (Spec Compliance Review, Step 4e) runs for all 4 units with COMPLIANT verdicts and requirement matrices. Stage 2 (Adversarial Review, Step 4f) runs for all 4 units with PASS verdicts, cheating detection, and coverage analysis. Stage 3 (Code Quality Review, Step 4g) runs for all 4 units with Approved assessments. The trace confirms the ordering: 'All 4 units pass spec compliance. Proceed to adversarial review.' then 'All 4 units pass adversarial review. Proceed to code quality review.' Each stage gates the next.

expectations.text: Runs a final integration test after all units complete
expectations.evidence: Trace Phase 5 (Final Review -- Integration Verification) runs after all 4 units complete. Step 1 runs the full test suite ('npx vitest run' -- all test files together) to catch import conflicts, shared state corruption, and missing shared dependencies. Step 2 verifies pristine output. Step 3 performs holistic code review for naming conflicts and missing connections. Step 4 performs cross-unit integration checking (POST creates what GET returns, DELETE removes what GET can no longer find). Step 5 applies anti-rationalization enforcement. The batch completion gate (Phase 4.4) confirms this only starts after all 4 units finish their full pipelines.

expectations.text: Decomposes into 4 work units with no dependencies between them
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'All four endpoints treated as one monolithic task.' Claude does not create discrete work units. It writes all four endpoints in a single file in a single pass. No WorkUnit data structure, no dependency analysis, and the explicit 'all endpoints are independent' hint is not operationalized.

expectations.text: Computes a single parallel batch containing all 4 units
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No batch computation; no concept of parallel batches.' No dependency DAG is built, no topological sort is performed, and no batch structure is computed. All four endpoints are processed linearly within a single thread.

expectations.text: Presents the execution plan showing all units run concurrently in Batch 1
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No execution plan presented; no concurrency.' Claude begins implementing immediately without presenting any plan to the user. There is no batch layout, no concurrency visualization, and no opportunity for user approval.

expectations.text: Dispatches multiple Test Writers simultaneously (up to maxParallelPairs config, default 3)
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No agent dispatch; single sequential thread.' No Test Writer agents are dispatched at all, let alone in parallel. The same single agent writes all implementation code and then all tests in sequence.

expectations.text: Each unit independently goes through the full RED->GREEN->review cycle
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No RED phase; tests written after all code.' Tests are written in a single pass after all four endpoints are implemented. No endpoint has an independent RED phase (failing tests) followed by a GREEN phase (passing implementation). Tests are never observed in a failing state.

expectations.text: Applies the three-stage review pipeline: spec compliance -> adversarial -> code quality
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'No review pipeline of any kind.' No spec compliance review verifies requirements. No adversarial review probes for edge cases or security issues. No code quality review checks for anti-patterns. Code is written, tests are added, tests pass, and the task is considered done.

expectations.text: Runs a final integration test after all units complete
expectations.evidence: The trace states: 'Runs jest once at the end, but this is the only test run, not a dedicated integration pass.' The single `jest` run executes individual endpoint tests but does not include deliberate cross-endpoint integration scenarios (e.g., POST then GET by ID, DELETE then verify absence in GET /users). There is no distinction between unit tests and a dedicated integration test pass.

notes: Iteration 3: validates SDD merge (Mode 4, code quality review, implementer dispatch, parallel batching)
All 7 with-skill evals at 100% — eval 5 regression fixed, new evals 6-7 pass cleanly
New features tested: plan execution, task classification, three-stage review, subagent status protocol, dependency graph batching
